ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has an entry for Centella asiatica as a food ingredient; no specific entry exists for pennywort salad as a prepared dish. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for pennywort salad as a prepared dish. A00LC (pennywort/Centella asiatica as a vegetable) is the closest defensible parent code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT has a substance-level entry for Centella asiatica; no prepared-dish concept exists. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of pennywort salad is approximately 120 g, typically presented as a small plate or bowl of dressed fresh pennywort leaves with condiments.

nutrient_source.title: Centella asiatica (pennywort / gotu kola), raw leaves — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table composite estimate
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for pennywort salad as a complete prepared dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central raw Centella asiatica / leafy-green herb data and FAO/INFOODS SEA regional vegetable composition data, adjusted for a typical light dressing (lime juice, fish sauce, small amount of oil). The dressing contributes modest sodium, minor fat, and minor carbohydrate increments. Values represent a best-estimate composite for the dressed salad per 100 g edible portion.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of non-starchy leafy vegetables and herb salads — analogous estimate based on raw leafy green vegetables (GI ≤ 15) per University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for pennywort salad exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Pennywort (Centella asiatica) is a non-starchy leafy herb with very low carbohydrate content (~5 g/100 g). GI is estimated at 15, consistent with the GI range for raw non-starchy leafy vegetables (GI 10–20) documented in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 120 g serving = (15 × 5.2 × 1.2) / 100 = 0.94, rounded to 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish sauce dressing
hypertension.guidance: Pennywort salad contains approximately 280 mg sodium per 100 g due to fish sauce or soy sauce in the dressing; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sauce preparation or limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake below 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low net carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 15 and only 5.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, pennywort salad has a negligible glycemic load and is well-suited for inclusion in a carbohydrate-controlled diet for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high antioxidant phytochemicals, and dietary fibre
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pennywort salad is low in saturated fat (0.3 g/100 g) and provides dietary fibre, vitamin C, and triterpenoid compounds from Centella asiatica that may support vascular health; the main cardiovascular caution is sodium from the dressing, which should be minimised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pennywort salad (မြင်းခွာရွက်သုပ်, Myin-kha-yar-ywet thoke) is a traditional Southeast Asian herbal salad made primarily from fresh leaves of Centella asiatica, commonly known as pennywort, gotu kola, or pegaga. The dish is particularly popular in Myanmar, where it is served as a light starter or side dish. The fresh leaves are tossed with a dressing of lime juice or vinegar, fish sauce or soy sauce, garlic, chilli, and shallots, and are often garnished with toasted sesame seeds or crushed peanuts. The salad has a distinctive slightly bitter, grassy, and herbaceous flavour with a crisp, tender texture. Centella asiatica is widely recognised in traditional medicine across South and Southeast Asia for its adaptogenic and wound-healing properties, attributed to triterpenoid compounds such as asiaticoside and madecassoside. Nutritionally, pennywort salad is low in calories (~45 kcal/100 g), low in carbohydrates (~5.2 g/100 g), and provides useful amounts of vitamin C (~48.5 mg/100 g), vitamin A (~98 mcg RAE/100 g), iron (~3.1 mg/100 g), calcium (~171 mg/100 g), and dietary fibre (~2.1 g/100 g). Its very low glycemic index (~15) makes it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The primary clinical caution is sodium from the dressing (approximately 280 mg/100 g), which is relevant for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. In Singapore, pennywort salad is served at select Southeast Asian and Burmese-style cafes and is also known by its Malay name salad pegaga and Tamil name vallarai salad.
common_user_queries: Is pennywort salad good for diabetes?
Is pegaga salad the same as pennywort salad?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for pennywort salad as a prepared dish exists in any single approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central raw Centella asiatica data and FAO/INFOODS SEA regional vegetable composition data, adjusted for a typical light dressing. GI is an analogous estimate based on non-starchy leafy vegetable GI ranges from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the primary ingredient (Centella asiatica) is well-characterised in approved sources and the dressing adjustments are minor and defensible.
notes: 1. Cuisine tag in the input JSON lists 'Italian / Pizza / Coffee / Toast' — this reflects the field survey restaurant category for The Light Cafe and does not represent the actual cuisine of the dish. Pennywort salad is a Burmese/Southeast Asian dish; cuisine has been corrected accordingly. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a minimally processed plant-based salad with no hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central leafy vegetable data. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based dish with no animal fat; confirmed zero per source. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based dish; no animal-derived B12 source present. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero for raw leafy vegetables not exposed to UV treatment; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central and FAO/INFOODS SEA data for Centella asiatica raw leaves; these are best-estimate values and should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis of the prepared dish. 7. Serving size of 120 g is an estimate for a standard restaurant portion; actual serving size may vary. 8. Allergen status: peanuts may be present as a garnish depending on preparation; fish sauce introduces fish allergen in non-vegan variants. 9. The Burmese script name မြင်းခွာရွက်သုပ်, preserved from the input record, is the standard Burmese name for this dish.
